question: The correct order of the spin –only magnetic moment of metal ions in the following low-spin complexes, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub><mo>]</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> is:
solution:
(i)  V+223⟹Ar3d34d0⟹  n=3 ⇒μ33+2=3.87
(ii) Cr+224⟹Ar3d4 n=2 ⇒μ22+2=2.82
(iii)   Ru+344⟹kr4d5    (n=1) ⇒μ11+2=3
(iv)   Fe+226 ⟹Ar3d6  (n=0) ⇒μ=0
Order of magnetic moment will be ∝ no. of unpaired e-
V+2> Cr+2>Ru+3 >Fe+2
μ=n(n+2) B.M. Hence more the value of n more will be magnitude moment.

Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Dimethyl glyoxime forms a six membered covalent chelate when treated with NiCl2 solution in presence of NH4OH.
Statement (II) : Prussian blue precipitate contains iron both in +2 and +3 oxidation states.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Dimethyl glyoxime forms a six membered covalent chelate when treated with NiCl2 solution in presence of NH4OH.
Statement (II) : Prussian blue precipitate contains iron both in +2 and +3 oxidation states.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
According to Statement I,

On addition of  dimethylglyoxime (DMG) to nickel ion solution and on adding  little bit of ammonia to make solution basic, it will give a red precipitate. This is a very specific test for nickel +2 cation.

The reaction is as follows,

Ni2++NH4OH+dmg→

2 hydrogen bonded chelatesare  formed.

According to Statement II,

IIIFe4 IIFeCN63

Prussian Blue

IN this complex Fe is  at +3 as well as +2 oxidation state. Where ionisation sphere is Fe3+ and coordination sphere has Fe2+

Hence option A is the answer..

q: The conductivity of a solution of complex with formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CoCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></math> corresponds to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> electrolyte, then the primary valency of central metal ion is
question: The conductivity of a solution of complex with formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CoCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></math> corresponds to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> electrolyte, then the primary valency of central metal ion is
solution:
Primary valencies are ionizable. If the complex ion exhibits positive charge, then primary valence corresponds to the number of charges present and is balanced by the same number of negative ions. Hence, primary valency can also be defined by the number of anions neutralizing the charge on the complex. 

CoNH34Cl2Cl

The above complex can be represented as CoNH34Cl2+Cl-. Hence, the primary valency of above complex is 3.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the correct statements from the following
A. $[Fe(C_2O_4)_3]^{3-}$ is the most stable complex among $[Fe(OH)_6]^{3-}$, $[Fe(C_2O_4)_3]^{3-}$ and $[Fe(SCN)_6]^{3-}$
B. The stability of $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$ is greater than that of $[Cu(en)_2]^{2+}$
C. The hybridization of Fe in $K_4[Fe(CN)_6]$ is $d^2sp^3$
D. $[Fe(NO_2)_3Cl_3]^{3-}$ exhibits linkage isomerism
E. $NO_2^-$ and $SCN^-$ ligands are NOT ambidentate ligands
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the correct statements from the following
A. $[Fe(C_2O_4)_3]^{3-}$ is the most stable complex among $[Fe(OH)_6]^{3-}$, $[Fe(C_2O_4)_3]^{3-}$ and $[Fe(SCN)_6]^{3-}$
B. The stability of $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$ is greater than that of $[Cu(en)_2]^{2+}$
C. The hybridization of Fe in $K_4[Fe(CN)_6]$ is $d^2sp^3$
D. $[Fe(NO_2)_3Cl_3]^{3-}$ exhibits linkage isomerism
E. $NO_2^-$ and $SCN^-$ ligands are NOT ambidentate ligands
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: A, B, C, D and E
solution:
Statement A is correct. The oxalate ion ($C_2O_4^{2-}$) is a bidentate ligand and forms chelate rings with the central metal ion. Due to the chelate effect, $[Fe(C_2O_4)_3]^{3-}$ is more stable than complexes formed by monodentate ligands like $[Fe(OH)_6]^{3-}$ and $[Fe(SCN)_6]^{3-}$.

Statement B is incorrect. Ethylenediamine (en) is a bidentate ligand and forms chelate rings, whereas $NH_3$ is a monodentate ligand. Therefore, $[Cu(en)_2]^{2+}$ is more stable than $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$ due to the chelate effect.

Statement C is correct. In $K_4[Fe(CN)_6]$, the oxidation state of Fe is $+2$, which corresponds to a $3d^6$ configuration. Since $CN^-$ is a strong field ligand, it causes the pairing of electrons in the $3d$ orbitals, leaving two inner $3d$ orbitals empty. Thus, the hybridization is $d^2sp^3$.

Statement D is correct. The $NO_2^-$ ligand is an ambidentate ligand, meaning it can coordinate to the metal center through either the nitrogen atom or the oxygen atom. Therefore, $[Fe(NO_2)_3Cl_3]^{3-}$ can exhibit linkage isomerism.

Statement E is incorrect. Both $NO_2^-$ and $SCN^-$ are ambidentate ligands. $NO_2^-$ can bind through N or O, and $SCN^-$ can bind through S or N.

Therefore, the correct statements are A, C, and D.

Answer: A, C and D only

question: When Ethane-1,2-diamine is added progressively to an aqueous solution of Nickel (II) chloride, the sequence of colour change observed will be :
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2}+\mathrm{en}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_4(\mathrm{en})\right]_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_4(\mathrm{en})\right]_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2}+\mathrm{en}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_2(\mathrm{en})_2\right]_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

Pale Blue
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_2(\mathrm{en})\right]_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2}+\mathrm{en}_{(\mathrm{aq})} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

Blue / purple

subject: Chemistry
q: One mole of the octahedral complex compound $\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}_3$ gives 3 moles of ions on dissolution in water. One mole of the same complex reacts with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution to yield two moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}$. The structure of the complex is:
question: One mole of the octahedral complex compound $\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}_3$ gives 3 moles of ions on dissolution in water. One mole of the same complex reacts with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution to yield two moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}$. The structure of the complex is:
solution: $\begin{array}{l}{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \underbrace{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{2+}(\text { aq. })+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\text {aq. })}_{3 \text { ions in water }}} \\ {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2(\text { aq. })+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_3(\text { aq. }) \rightarrow} {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right]\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2(\text { aq. })+2 \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})}\end{array}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The observed magnetic moment of the complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>Mn</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>NCS</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>)</mo><msup><mo>]</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>06</mn><mo> </mo><mi>BM</mi></math>. The numerical value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
question: The observed magnetic moment of the complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>Mn</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>NCS</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>)</mo><msup><mo>]</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>06</mn><mo> </mo><mi>BM</mi></math>. The numerical value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
The complex [Mn(NCS)6)]x- has a magnetic moment of 6.06 BM. This indicates that the complex has unpaired electrons, which contribute to its magnetic moment.

Number of unpaired electrons =5(Mn2+ : 3d5).
So Mn must be in +2 oxidation state Mn2+.

⇒ 2 + (–6) = –x ⇒ –4 = –x ⇒ x = 4
The numerical value of x is 4, so the complex is [Mn(NCS)6)]4-.

q: An octahedral complex of Co<sup>3</sup><sup>+</sup> is diamagnetic. The hybridisation involved in the formation of the complex is :
question: An octahedral complex of Co<sup>3</sup><sup>+</sup> is diamagnetic. The hybridisation involved in the formation of the complex is :
solution: 2 7 Co 3 +  has 24 Electrons i.e 6 electrons in inner 3d orbital of central metal ion for diamagnetic behaviour these d6 Electrons have to be paired and due to pairing two inner d-orbitals get empty and hybridisation is d2sp3.

subject: Chemistry
q:
X is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right) \mathrm{BrCl}\right]$.
Y is the number of optically inactive isomer(s) exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_{2}(\mathrm{ox})_{2}\right]^{3-}$
Z is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)_{3}\right]$.
The value of $\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}+\mathrm{Z}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question:
X is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right) \mathrm{BrCl}\right]$.
Y is the number of optically inactive isomer(s) exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_{2}(\mathrm{ox})_{2}\right]^{3-}$
Z is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)_{3}\right]$.
The value of $\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}+\mathrm{Z}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For $[Pt(NH_3)(H_2O)BrCl]$ with square planar geometry and four different ligands, geometrical isomerism arises from different positional arrangements: X = 3.
For $[CrCl_2(ox)_2]^{3-}$, the trans isomer is optically inactive (meso compound) while cis is optically active, giving Y = 1.
For $[Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3]$ in octahedral geometry, facial (fac) and meridional (mer) isomers are possible: Z = 2.
Therefore, X + Y + Z = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6

subject: Chemistry
q: The total number of possible isomers for squareplanar $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{Cl})\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)(\mathrm{SCN})\right]^{2-}$ is:
question: The total number of possible isomers for squareplanar $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{Cl})\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)(\mathrm{SCN})\right]^{2-}$ is:
solution:
The square planar complex of the type $[\mathrm{Mabcd}]^{\mathrm{n} \pm}$, where all four ligands are different, has 3 geometrical isomers. But if one of the ligands is ambidentate, then $2 \times$ $3=6$ geometrical isomers are possible. But if two ligands are ambidentate, then $4 \times 3=$ 12 geometrical isomers are possible.
In the given example, $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$and $\mathrm{SCN}^{-}$are ambidentate ligands.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List I with List II:

List I
(Complexes)		 List II
(Hybridisation)
(A) Ni(CO)4 I sp3
(B) CuNH342+ II dsp2
(C) FeNH362+ III sp3d2
(D) FeH2O62+ IV d2sp3
question:
Match List I with List II:

List I
(Complexes)		 List II
(Hybridisation)
(A) Ni(CO)4 I sp3
(B) CuNH342+ II dsp2
(C) FeNH362+ III sp3d2
(D) FeH2O62+ IV d2sp3
options: A – II, B – I, C – III, D – IV
A – I, B – II, C – III, D – IV
A – II, B – I, C – IV, D – III
A – I, B– II, C – IV, D – III
solution:
Ni is in zero oxidation state in [Ni(CO)4]. So, the electronic configuration of Ni is 3d8 4s2. As CO is a strong ligand, it pushes all the electrons in the 3d orbital, therefore the hybridisation of [Ni(CO)4] is sp3 and it has tetrahedral geometry. It is diamagnetic due to the absence of unpaired electrons.

CuNH342+ ion, oxidation state of Cu is +2 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d9.  So, there would be a rearrangement of electrons in Cu2+ because of the NH3 strong field ligand . And the last electron in the d-orbital would be out waiting for the N's electrons to fill up first. So, the 4 electron pairs from N would be in one 3d, one 4s, & two 4p orbitals and in the third place of 4p orbital, the e- from 3d would take place. So, you can say the hybridisation here would be dsp2.

FeNH362+ ion, oxidation state of Fe is +2 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d6. NH3 is strong field ligand with complex. So, no pairing will be there. So, you can say the hybridisation here would be d2sp3.

 

FeH2O62+ ion, oxidation state of Fe is +2 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d6. There are 4 unpaired electrons in 3d orbital.  So, you can say the hybridisation here would be sp3d2.

So, correct order of matching is given in the below table:

List I
(Complexes)		 List II
(Hybridisation)
Ni(CO)4 sp3
CuNH342+ dsp2
FeNH362+ d2sp3
FeH2O62+ sp3d2

Possible Structural isomers No of geometrical isomer by each
MClFNO2SCN 3

q: Which of the following will have maximum stabilization due to crystal field?
question: Which of the following will have maximum stabilization due to crystal field?
solution:
CoH2O62+; t2g6eg0; CFSE =-0.4×6+0.6×1Δo+2P=−1.8Δo+2P

CoCN63-; t2g6eg0; CFSE =-0.4×6+0.6×0Δo+2P=−2.4Δo+2P

Since CN-1 is a strong field ligand, Δo of this complex will be higher than that of CoH2O63+

subject: Chemistry
q: The conditions and consequence that favours the $t_{2 g}^3 e_g^1$ configuration in a metal complex are
question: The conditions and consequence that favours the $t_{2 g}^3 e_g^1$ configuration in a metal complex are
options: weak field ligand, low spin complex
weak field ligand, high spin complex
strong field ligand, high spin complex
strong field ligand, low spin complex
solution:
The conditions and consequence that favour $t_{2 g}^3 e_g^1$ configuration in a metal complex are
(i) weak field ligand, and
(ii) high spin complex

For weak field ligands, splitting energy ( $\Delta_0$ ) is lower than pairing energy $(P)$. As a result distribution of electrons for $3 \mathrm{~d}^4$ will be $t_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^3 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^1$. It results in high spin complex due to maximum number of unpaired electrons.

q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : In octahedral complexes, when \(\Delta_{\mathrm{o}} \lt \mathrm{P}\) high spin complexes are formed. When \(\Delta_{\mathrm{o}} \gt \mathrm{P}\) low spin complexes are formed.
Statement (II) : In tetrahedral complexes because of \(\Delta_{\mathrm{t}} \lt \mathrm{P}\), low spin complexes are rarely formed.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : In octahedral complexes, when \(\Delta_{\mathrm{o}} \lt \mathrm{P}\) high spin complexes are formed. When \(\Delta_{\mathrm{o}} \gt \mathrm{P}\) low spin complexes are formed.
Statement (II) : In tetrahedral complexes because of \(\Delta_{\mathrm{t}} \lt \mathrm{P}\), low spin complexes are rarely formed.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
solution:
In octahedral complex \((\mathrm{CN}=6)\)
If \(\Delta_0 \lt \) P.E., then high spin complexes are formed
If \(\Delta_0 \gt \) P.E., then low spin complexes are formed But in tetrahedral complex \((\mathrm{CN}=4)\)
\(\Delta_t \lt \) P.E., then mainly high spin complexes are formed and rarely low spin complexes are formed.

question:

Among the given complexes, number of paramagnetic complexes is
solution:
Complexes in which central atom has unpaired electrons is said to be paramagnetic in nature and where unpaired electrons are not present are diamagnetic in nature.

FeCN63- ,   Fe+3  3d5 → t2g2,2  eg1, paramagnetic (one unpaired electron)

FeCN64-,  Fe+2 3d6→t2g6eg0 ,diamagnetic 

TiCN63-,  Ti+3(3d1)→t2g1 , Paramagnetic (1 unpaired electron)

NiCN42-, Ni+2(3d8)→ Diamagnetic

CoCN63-,   Co+3(3d6)→t2g6, diamagnetic

q: Total number of moles of AgCl precipitated on addition of excess of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> to one mole each of the following complexes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi>Cl</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pt</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pd</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is
question: Total number of moles of AgCl precipitated on addition of excess of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> to one mole each of the following complexes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi>Cl</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pt</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Pd</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is
solution:
Silver nitrate on reaction of chloride ion gives silver chloride as precipitate. Chloride ions outside of coordination sphere will only give precipitates. Thus,
CoNH34Cl2Cl⇒ Gives 1 mole AgCl
NiH2O6Cl2⇒ Gives 2 moles AgCl
PtNH32Cl2⇒ Gives No AgCl
PdNH34Cl2⇒ Gives 2 moles AgCl
Total number of moles of AgCl=5 mole.

subject: Chemistry
q: Total number of unpaired electrons present in the central metal atoms/ions of $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right],\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Total number of unpaired electrons present in the central metal atoms/ions of $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right],\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$[Ni(CO)_4]$:
Ni is zero-valent with configuration $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^2$.
CO is a strong field ligand causing complete pairing in the filled $3d^{10}$ orbital.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[NiCl_4]^{2-}$:
Ni is $+2$ with configuration $[Ar]3d^8$.
Cl is a weak field ligand producing high-spin tetrahedral geometry with configuration $3d^8$: five orbitals with two electrons paired, three electrons unpaired.
Unpaired electrons = 2.

$[PtCl_2(NH_3)_2]$:
Pt is $+2$ with configuration $[Xe]4f^{14}5d^8$.
Pt(II) adopts square planar geometry with strong-field splitting leading to complete pairing: $5d^8$ configuration places all electrons in bonding orbitals.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$:
Ni is $+2$ with $3d^8$ configuration.
CN is a strong field ligand.
Square planar geometry is preferred, resulting in all electrons paired.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

$[Pt(CN)_4]^{2-}$:
Pt is $+2$ with $5d^8$ configuration.
CN is a strong field ligand producing square planar geometry with complete pairing.
Unpaired electrons = 0.

Total unpaired electrons = 0 + 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 2.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> cation gives a prussian blue precipitate on addition of potassium ferrocyanide solution due to the formation of
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> cation gives a prussian blue precipitate on addition of potassium ferrocyanide solution due to the formation of
solution:
Ferric ion forms a prussian blue precipitate due to the formation of Fe4FeCN63 . In this method, to a portion of sodium fusion extract, freshly prepared ferrous sulphate FeSO4 solution is added and warmed. Then about 2 to 3 drops of FeCl3 solution are added and acidified with conc. HCl . The appearance of a prussian blue colour indicates the presence of nitrogen.

FeSO4+2NaOH→FeOH2+Na2SO4

6NaCN+Fe ( OH ) 2 → N a 4 [ Fe ( CN ) 6 ] Sodium ferrocyanide +2NaOH

3N a 4 [ Fe ( CN ) 6 ]+4FeC l 3 → F e 4 [ Fe ( CN ) 6 ] 3 Ferric ferrocyanide ( prussian blue ) +12NaCl
